title: Containment assertions hide encoding corruption
description: The REST APPEND/SETRANGE/GETSET handlers JSON-encoded string operands while SET stored them raw, so `SET ab` + `APPEND cd` produced `ab"cd"`. It shipped in 1.0.0 and survived review because the tests could not see it: test_string_append asserted `stored_value.contains("hello")`, which is equally true of the corrupted `"hello"`. Worse, the GETSET test pinned the corruption as expected output (assert_eq!(value_new, "\"initial\"")) two lines after asserting the un-quoted form for a SET-written value — the test contradicted itself and was trusted anyway. Rules: (1) for anything involving encoding/serialization, assert full equality, never `contains`; (2) when two assertions in one test disagree about the same data's shape, that is a bug report, not a quirk to encode; (3) put an encode/decode rule in ONE helper with its inverse documented next to it — four handlers re-implementing it is what let them drift. Fixed in 8e5b4f9 via encode_value_bytes/decode_stored_value.
